title: Cats and Catnip 🐱 The Science of Nepetalactone
description: Why does catnip drive cats wild? Discover nepetalactone, the chemical compound responsible. Only 70% of cats are sensitive to it. Kittens are immune, seniors lose interest. The biology, the genetics, and the surprising science behind this strange plant.

segments.voice: Catnip has fascinated humans for centuries. Drop a pinch of dried leaves on the floor and watch what happens. Some cats roll, some drool, some sprint around the house like the floor is on fire. But what's actually going on inside their brain? The answer is chemistry.

Welcome to CatBrothers. The compound responsible has a name that sounds straight out of a lab notebook — nepetalactone. It was first identified in 1941 by a chemist named Samuel McElvain at the University of Wisconsin. Cats had been hooked on it for thousands of years before he ever isolated the molecule.

Nepetalactone is a secondary metabolite of the plant Nepeta cataria. The plant doesn't produce it for cats. It produces it as a defense weapon against insects, especially aphids and mosquitoes. The fact that cats love it is a beautiful evolutionary coincidence.

When a cat sniffs catnip, the molecule binds to receptors in the nose. From there, it travels straight to the olfactory bulb and on to brain regions tied to reward and reproductive behavior. That's why the response often looks like a mix of euphoria and mating posture.

Here's the catch. Only about 70% of cats are sensitive to it. The trait is inherited, controlled by a single dominant gene. If your cat ignores catnip, they're not broken. They're just genetically immune. It's perfectly normal.

Cats that do react usually start showing sensitivity around 8 to 12 months of age. Kittens younger than 8 weeks are completely immune. Their olfactory system simply isn't mature enough to register the molecule. Try it on a 6-week-old kitten and you'll get a confused stare.

The classic catnip response includes face rubbing, drooling, leaf chewing, purring, and rolling. Some cats turn aggressive — pupils dilated, attacking invisible enemies. Others zone out completely, sedated and dreamy. Same compound, opposite reactions.

The high lasts between 5 and 15 minutes. After that, the cat enters a refractory period. Offer fresh catnip during this window — nothing happens. The brain stops responding entirely. It's nature's built-in safety brake.

Recovery takes 30 minutes to 2 hours. During that gap, the liver metabolizes the nepetalactone and the receptors reset. After roughly an hour, sensitivity returns and the cat can ride the wave all over again.

The temporary tolerance isn't because the plant lost potency. It's the cat's nervous system protecting itself from overstimulation. Researchers see this kind of feedback loop in many natural compound responses across mammals.

A 2021 study from Iwate University in Japan found something remarkable. Nepetalactone, once rubbed onto a cat's fur, repels mosquitoes. The team concluded that face-rubbing on catnip might literally be the cat dosing itself with insect repellent — for free.

That changes the picture entirely. The catnip reaction isn't pure recreation. It may be self-medication. A wild cat rubbing on Nepeta plants is essentially applying bug spray, an evolutionary perk we only just figured out.

There are alternatives for non-responders. Silver vine, valerian root, and Tatarian honeysuckle all trigger similar effects through related compounds. Each plant activates a slightly different cocktail of receptors.

A study by Sebastiaan Bol at Cowboy Cat Ranch tested all four plants on 100 cats. Around 79% of cats responded to silver vine — including roughly 75% of the cats that ignored catnip. So if your cat skips catnip, silver vine has a strong chance of working.

Big cats fall for it, too. Lions, tigers, leopards, and jaguars all show the same rolling, rubbing, drooling response when exposed to catnip. The sensitivity is ancient. It's been baked into the feline lineage for millions of years.

Senior cats often lose their interest. Cats older than 12 to 15 years frequently stop reacting altogether. The olfactory receptors become less responsive with age, and brain reward circuits weaken. It's not that the catnip changed — it's the cat that changed.

Catnip is safe. It's not addictive in any meaningful sense — there's no withdrawal, no dependency, no physical craving. Vets routinely recommend it as enrichment, especially for indoor cats who need extra stimulation.

So catnip isn't magic. It's chemistry, evolution, and a happy accident dating back to a plant trying to kill bugs. Next time your cat goes wild for a green pile of leaves, remember — they're not just having fun. They might be applying a million-year-old bug repellent.
